result:
Lately I’ve been thinking about how much of life is made out of small thresholds.

A door is the obvious one: you are in one room, then another. But there are quieter doors everywhere. The moment before you send a message. The second before you apologize. The almost-invisible pause between having an idea and deciding it is worth following. Even waking up is a threshold: for a few seconds you are not fully the person with obligations and memory and plans; you are just consciousness returning to its address.

We tend to remember the large crossings—moving cities, beginning or ending relationships, changing careers—but the small ones accumulate into the shape of a life. You become brave by repeatedly stepping over tiny lines. You become distant the same way. You become good at something by entering, again and again, the little doorway between not practicing and practicing.

There is a strange mercy in this. If we were required to transform all at once, almost no one would manage it. But most change arrives disguised as a minor decision: drink water, take the walk, tell the truth a little earlier, read one page, stop before saying the cruel thing, start before feeling ready.

I like the idea that a day is not a block of time but a corridor full of handles. Some open onto familiar rooms. Some are locked. Some stick. Some lead outside.

And sometimes the most important thing is not to force a grand entrance, but simply to notice: here is a threshold. Here is the next small crossing. Here is where I can choose.

Lately I’ve been thinking about how much of life is made out of small thresholds.

A door is the obvious one: you are in one room, then another. But there are quieter doors everywhere. The moment before you send a message. The second before you apologize. The almost-invisible pause between having an idea and deciding it is worth following. Even waking up is a threshold: for a few seconds you are not fully the person with obligations and memory and plans; you are just consciousness returning to its address.

We tend to remember the large crossings—moving cities, beginning or ending relationships, changing careers—but the small ones accumulate into the shape of a life. You become brave by repeatedly stepping over tiny lines. You become distant the same way. You become good at something by entering, again and again, the little doorway between not practicing and practicing.

There is a strange mercy in this. If we were required to transform all at once, almost no one would manage it. But most change arrives disguised as a minor decision: drink water, take the walk, tell the truth a little earlier, read one page, stop before saying the cruel thing, start before feeling ready.

I like the idea that a day is not a block of time but a corridor full of handles. Some open onto familiar rooms. Some are locked. Some stick. Some lead outside.

And sometimes the most important thing is not to force a grand entrance, but simply to notice: here is a threshold. Here is the next small crossing. Here is where I can choose.
